perturbation.original_text: Developer shall be fully liable, without limitation, for money damages resulting from its improper or unauthorized use, modification, alteration, licensing or transfer of the Technology or Improvements, or resulting from its failure to provide functional and merchantable Improvements hereunder, which failure shall be deemed a material breach of this Agreement by Developer.
perturbation.changed_text: Developer shall be reasonably liable for damages resulting from its use, modification, alteration, licensing or transfer of the Technology or Improvements, or resulting from its failure to provide Improvements hereunder, which failure may be deemed a breach of this Agreement by Developer.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces 'fully liable, without limitation' with 'reasonably liable,' introducing ambiguity regarding the extent of liability. 'Improper or unauthorized' is removed, further weakening the obligation. Replacing 'functional and merchantable Improvements' with just 'Improvements' reduces the standard of performance, and changing 'shall be deemed a material breach' to 'may be deemed a breach' makes the consequences discretionary.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract Laws in New York State
perturbation.law_explanation: New York contract law, while allowing for limitation of liability, requires clear and unambiguous language. The modified clause uses vague terms like 'reasonably liable' and weakens the breach condition, making it difficult to enforce specific performance or claim damages, especially concerning the delivery of functional or merchantable improvements. This ambiguity makes it harder to prove the extent of liability, potentially conflicting with the principle of clear contractual obligations under New York law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: o file a claim and/or commence an action on a payment bond on exhaustion of another legal remedy is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this subdivision shall not affect the validity of any insu
perturbation.justification: The original contract language requires the 'Developer' to be 'fully liable, without limitation.' The modified language changes this to 'reasonably liable.' This introduces ambiguity regarding the extent of the 'Developer's' liability. This ambiguity makes it difficult to enforce specific performance or claim damages. Snippet 1 is not directly applicable because it speaks to filing a claim or action on a payment bond. Snippet 2 is empty. The change from 'shall be deemed a material breach' to 'may be deemed a breach' also weakens the original legal obligation.

perturbation.original_text: All notices sent under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed effectively given (i) upon personal delivery to the party to be notified; (ii) when sent by e-mail PDF or confirmed facsimile, if sent during normal business hours of the recipient, if not, then on the next business day; (iii) three (3) days after having been sent by registered or certified mail, return receipt requested, postage prepaid; or (iv) two (2) days after deposit with an internationally recognized overnight courier, specifying two (2) day delivery, with written verification of receipt.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices sent under this Agreement should be in writing and may be deemed effectively given (i) upon personal delivery; (ii) when sent by e-mail, if convenient; (iii) after having been sent by mail; or (iv) after deposit with a courier.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces substantial ambiguity. Changing 'shall' to 'should' makes the written notice requirement discretionary. Replacing 'shall be deemed effectively given' with 'may be deemed effectively given' makes the effectiveness of notice uncertain. Removing specific requirements for email (PDF, confirmed facsimile, business hours) and mail (registered, certified, return receipt requested, postage prepaid) and courier (internationally recognized, 2-day delivery, written verification) further weakens the clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Frauds in New York
perturbation.law_explanation: The Statute of Frauds in New York requires certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable. Ambiguous notice provisions create uncertainty about whether proper notification occurred, which could invalidate contract-related actions relying on such notice. The lack of specificity regarding delivery methods (e.g., certified mail for proof) and the discretionary language ('may be deemed') undermine the requirement for clear written communication, potentially rendering critical contractual actions unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds. The less stringent requirements on email and courier service further dilute the enforceability of the notice provisions.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability up TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability next SECTION 5-702 Requirements for use of plain language in consumer transactions This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates specific methods for delivering notices, ensuring proof of receipt and timely delivery, whereas the changed contract introduces ambiguity and weakens these requirements. The Statute of Frauds, as suggested by the LLM's Law Explanation, requires certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable. The changed text undermines the clarity of notice provisions, which could lead to disputes about whether proper notification occurred. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses agreements needing to be in writing under NY law (specifically NY CLS Gen Oblig § 5-701), this provides general context that highlights the importance of clear contractual obligations. The original text aims to fulfill this requirement through its detailed stipulations. The changed text weakens that original intent, and could create an ambiguity that leads to an unenforceable contract.

perturbation.original_text: It is hereby irrevocably agreed that legal jurisdiction and venue for any proceeding arising out of this Agreement shall be in the state or federal courts located in the County of Monroe, State of New York, United States.
perturbation.changed_text: It is agreed that legal jurisdiction and venue for any proceeding arising out of this Agreement should preferably be in the courts located in the State of New York, United States.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause establishes a mandatory and specific jurisdiction (Monroe County, New York). The modification introduces ambiguity by replacing 'irrevocably agreed' with 'agreed,' removing the certainty. Replacing 'shall be' with 'should preferably be' makes the jurisdiction a preference rather than a requirement. Removing 'state or federal courts located in the County of Monroe' broadens the jurisdiction to the entire state and removes the county, increasing the vagueness.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Law regarding Forum Selection Clauses
perturbation.law_explanation: While 28 U.S.C. § 1404(a) allows for transfer of venue for the convenience of parties and witnesses, a clear and unambiguous forum selection clause is generally enforced unless it is unreasonable or unjust. By making the venue 'preferable' rather than mandatory, the modified clause undermines the certainty required for a valid forum selection clause, potentially conflicting with the established legal preference for enforcing such clauses unless strong reasons exist to the contrary. The ambiguity could lead a court to disregard the intended forum, increasing litigation costs and uncertainty.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity regarding the location of jurisdiction, weakening the original clause's clear designation of Monroe County, New York. The change from 'shall be' to 'should preferably be' undermines the mandatory nature of the forum selection clause. The Law Explanation states that a 'clear and unambiguous forum selection clause is generally enforced unless it is unreasonable or unjust.' By making the venue 'preferable' rather than mandatory, the modified clause conflicts with the established legal preference for enforcing such clauses, as now there are strong reasons to disregard the intended forum, increasing litigation costs and uncertainty. The change makes the legal obligation ambiguous. This aligns with the definition of 'Ambiguities Legal Contradiction', making it difficult to enforce the legal obligation under state or national law.
